Try and come up with one of each for your business and help each other. So a really good rope them in and a real good deny the negative statement. It doesn't really matter whether it's for a particular product, whether it's for connection with you in general, whether it's for the next sales letter you happen to be working on, doesn't make any difference. But let's see who can come up with really good ones and please help each other. And please remember to rotate out so you're not always sitting with the very same people. And I think that's pretty important. Let's take 15 minutes. Oh, and Tucker, I need your watch. This is a good example of something. And most of you won't. But with this and many of the other things we have done here in 10 minutes or 15 minutes or 20 minutes, you are well advised to go home and spend more time on this. Because really like in this particular category, what you want is a little pre done inventory of these things as templates. That again you can pull out, kind of fill in the blanks for yourself and use again and again and again and again. And again, they need to be business specific. They need to be specific to your market. But you don't want to be sitting down every time you're going to crank out a newsletter or every time you're going to crank out a marketing piece and trying to do all this from scratch. And so an hour spent on this in advance is worth hundreds of hours over time. And I would even say for, you know, we listed all the reasons why people buy, it's probably good to have one or two of these for every single reason why people would or wouldn't buy. Yeah. See the reason, like as a copywriter, the reason that with each passing year I tried to take as little as I can outside of a few categories is because to do it right you need a big bank of this stuff for that category. And so if I got to start from scratch with something I've not done before, the workload is monstrous if you're going to do it right. Because you got to go get their language right. You got to go figure out what these things are for their industry. Or if I'm going to do weight loss again or I'm going to do a bootcamp campaign for somebody, see it doesn't matter whether it's property, you know, see 80% of it, to get them to a bootcamp is getting them to a bootcamp and it doesn't matter a whole hell of a lot what the bootcamp is and I can rely on the client for language nuances, right. But if I got to go do his stuff with hydraulics, I mean for ten times the workload. Because I got to go build all this shit from scratch and then I'm only probably going to get to use it once. So, you know, it's not a good money deal for me. I got a job recently and I want to be a copywriter. It can't recommend it to me. And I got an email from her just while I was here a couple of days ago. And she said after spending 60 plus hours on the job, she was returning my package to me because she felt she couldn't just, she couldn't get it. Well, let me tell you something. You got to give her big points. And I'd be a generous soul and pay her a kill fee. But, and I might try and get her to go back to work because that's a rare burst of honesty amongst our particular Gen of Thieves. I mean, see, most copywriters, the problem with hiring copywriters is most copywriters view the job of writing copy as writing copy. And they go from you giving them an assignment and your little pile of stuff to writing copy. And that's the smallest part of the job. The biggest part of the job is all this kind of stuff that we're doing to get ready to write the copy. And most of them don't ever do any of this stuff. So you get sort of one size fits all cut and pasted stuff. Now, in some environments, it's good enough because it's going to people that have never seen anything good at all. So, you know, if the only thing anybody's ever seen in their entire life is a dog and a cat and a zebra shows up in the parking lot, well, to them, that's like the whole Barnum and Bailey circus showing up. I mean, everybody runs out in the parking lot and looks at the zebra. But if they've like been to the zoo 8, 10, 12 times, see that's no good. The zebra comes in the parking lot. Everybody looks out the window and says, oh, there's a zebra in the parking lot. Back to work, right? So, I mean, a lot of it depends on who you're going to and what they've seen, what you can get away with. But see, most copywriters do not do this work. They just write. And that's why largely you can take their stuff over a 10-year period of time, lay it all out on a table, and really it's pretty much all the same shit. But I give her, it doesn't solve your problem, by the way, but I give her big points for honesty. She could have taken your money. Page 133, principle 20 is a favorite of mine. It's called the principle of transparency. My preference is always not to have skeletons in the closet. And often there's opportunity in using them. But even when there isn't, you are generally better off trotting out the elephant rather than hoping nobody notices it. And just about everybody has some collection of flaws in their argument, their proposition, their cause, their product in themselves. And when they drip out to a group of people you are trying to influence over time, they do untold amounts of damage. When they're all put out ahead of time, they often do no damage at all and can sometimes be turned to the good. So if you watch like now our current political campaign, see the guys who don't get this are starting to bleed through little pinpricks. Because this thing is being disclosed this week. Now next week something else is going to be disclosed. And next week something else is going to be disclosed. And now the entire conversation is about the next thing and the next thing and the next thing and the next thing. And the person loses total control of what the conversation is supposed to be about. You could just as easily put all ten of them things on the table to start with, had one really bad week and moved on and recaptured the control of the conversation. So one of the things to talk about copyrighting that I usually talk to clients about is what's all the bad shit. I mean what's wrong with this deal and why don't people buy it. So like to go back to the exercise we just did. A big commonality of course in a lot of information businesses to niches, get rich stuff, health and fitness stuff, right, is that the vast majority of the people don't get any results. Because of course in most cases the vast majority of the people don't do anything, right. Well you can ignore that if you like, but eventually you're going to have people at a seminar compare notes and three, four some aren't getting any results because they aren't doing anything. Or you're going to have the, which by the way is a hint because it keeps happening to me when I guest on people's tele-seminars. They ought not to be loose to talk to each other before the call starts for ten minutes. It's a real bad idea. And so I've been on, I can't count the number of them where I call in early I'm on the wrong side and here's what's going on. You know this is Billy Bob from Omaha. Is there anybody on here who's making any money with this thing? Oh this is Susie from Texas and nah I haven't done anything. Anybody done it and one after the other after the other after the other. Well after ten minutes of this you might as well not do the tele-seminar, right.
